beats.prompt: Back in Expected Value you found the balance point of a fair die. Match each statement to its value.

pairs.left: EV's last lesson said ___ is next

feedback.correct: E[X]=7/2 is the balance point; the spread around it is variance.
hints: Both came from Expected Value — the die mean and the 'coming next' promise.
(1+2+3+4+5+6)/6 = 21/6 = 7/2; EV closed promising a spread number.
7/2; the spread = variance.

beats.prompt: Two carnival games both pay $5 on average. Game A pays exactly $5 every time. Game B pays $0 or $10 on a fair coin flip. Same expected value — which game is riskier?

options: Same mean ⇒ same risk — neither is riskier
The 0/$10 game is riskier — bigger spread

Same mean ⇒ same risk — neither is riski.note: Let's test it — both average $5, but the coin-flip game can pay $0 or $10. Same balance point, very different spread. We need a second number.

The fixed-$5 game is riskier.note: Let's test it — backwards: a guaranteed $5 has zero spread. The 0/$10 game is the volatile one.

The 0/$10 game is riskier — bigger sprea.note: Good instinct — equal means, but spread around the mean differs. That spread is what variance measures.

beats.prompt: Quick check before we calculate — E[X²] and E[X]² are different things.

interaction.title: E[X²] ≠ E[X]²
interaction.body:
E[X²] = mean of the squares: square each outcome, then weight and sum.
E[X]² = square of the mean: compute E[X] first, then square.

For a fair die: E[X²] = (1²+2²+3²+4²+5²+6²)/6 = 91/6 ≈ 15.17, but E[X]² = (7/2)² = 49/4 = 12.25. These are not equal — confusing them is the most common variance mistake.

feedback.correct: Mean of squares ≠ square of the mean — the whole point of variance is the gap between them.
hints: Read the card.
E[X²]: square each value, then average. E[X]²: average first, then square.
Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: Tap each face value to place its probability weight. Watch the spread grow around the fixed fulcrum at E[X]=7/2.

hero.structuralReadout: E[X]=7/2 · Var=35/12 — same balance point, different spread.

feedback.correct: Same balance point, different spread — that's variance.
hints: Tap each number to place its weight (1/6 for a fair die).
The fulcrum stays pinned at 7/2 while the spread grows.
Place all six weights and watch the spread readout settle at 35/12.

beats.prompt: Given E[X²]=91/6 and E[X]²=(7/2)²=49/4, compute Var(X) = E[X²] − E[X]². Enter as a fraction.

feedback.correct: Yes — Var = E[X²] − E[X]² = 35/12. Spread around the mean, not from zero.
hints: Variance needs two pieces — mean-of-squares and square-of-mean; you have both.
E[X²]=91/6, E[X]²=49/4; convert to twelfths and subtract — don't stop at 91/6.

beats.interviewNote: For the interview: Var(X)=E[(X−μ)²] is the definition by squared deviation; E[X²]−E[X]² is the computational shortcut proved by expanding the square. Both forms appear in practice — the shortcut is faster for pmf computations; the deviation form is more interpretable as 'average squared distance from the mean'. SD=√Var puts spread back in the original units (dollars, meters, etc.), which is why SD is reported more often than variance in applied statistics.
beats.prompt: Three ways to see the same formula — tap each lens.

lenses.label: Definition
lenses.body: Variance is the average squared distance from the mean: Var(X) = E[(X−μ)²]. Squaring makes all distances positive and penalizes large deviations more.

lenses.label: Shortcut
lenses.body: Expand (X−μ)² = X²−2μX+μ² and take expectations: E[X²]−2μ²+μ² = E[X²]−E[X]². Mean-of-squares minus square-of-mean.

lenses.label: Units
lenses.body: Variance is in squared units (dollars², meters²). SD = √Var(X) = √(35/12) ≈ 1.71 returns spread to the die's own units — but √(35/12) is irrational, so we grade the rational Var=35/12.

hints: Tap each lens card to reveal its perspective.
All three lenses describe the same quantity from different angles.
The converge line: Var(X) = E[X²] − E[X]².

beats.prompt: Transfer challenge: a FAIR tetrahedral die shows 1, 2, 3, or 4, each with probability 1/4. Build E[X²] from the pmf, then compute Var(X) = E[X²] − E[X]². Enter both as fractions.

interaction.scenario: A four-sided die is rolled; outcomes 1, 2, 3, 4 are each equally likely (probability 1/4). Step 1: build E[X²] = Σ x²·P(x) yourself. Step 2: with E[X]=5/2, apply Var(X) = E[X²] − E[X]². Keep squared units — do not take a square root.

feedback.correct: Built E[X²]=(1+4+9+16)/4=30/4=15/2 from the pmf, then Var=15/2−(5/2)²=30/4−25/4=5/4. Same move as the six-sided die, fresh numbers.
hints: Two steps: build mean-of-squares from the pmf yourself, then apply Var = E[X²] − E[X]².
E[X²]=(1²+2²+3²+4²)/4=(1+4+9+16)/4=30/4=15/2; E[X]=(1+2+3+4)/4=5/2 so E[X]²=25/4; convert E[X²] to quarters and subtract — don't take a root.

beats.prompt: Mastery: derive E[X²] from the fair-die pmf from scratch, then compute Var(X). Enter both as fractions.

interaction.scenario: A fair die has outcomes 1–6, each with probability 1/6. Step 1: build E[X²] = Σ x²·P(x) yourself. Step 2: apply Var(X) = E[X²] − E[X]², using E[X]=7/2.

feedback.correct: Nailed both — built E[X²]=91/6 from the pmf, then subtracted to 35/12.
hints: Two steps: build mean-of-squares yourself, then apply the definition.
E[X²]=(1²+2²+3²+4²+5²+6²)/6 = (1+4+9+16+25+36)/6 = 91/6; Var=91/6−49/4; keep squared units — don't take a root.

beats.prompt: The move that ran through this whole lesson.

feedback.correct: Variance = spread²: Var(X) = E[X²] − E[X]². SD = √Var puts spread back in the variable's units. Next: two variables moving together — covariance.
hints: Read each recap card.
Var(X) = E[X²] − E[X]² — mean-of-squares minus square-of-mean.
Tap to continue.
